claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 9 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 9 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 9 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to know some basic information about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

The eligibility depends on the child's age:
- **Under 16**: Children under 16 generally qualify (with some conditions)
- **16-19**: May qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, let me know their ages too!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I have one child - Alex, who's 9 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 9 years old.

Since Alex is under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to check a few more conditions.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is only 9 years old, I expect the answer is that Alex is not in paid work at all. This is just a standard check in the eligibility process.

Can you confirm that Alex is not working 24 or more hours per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - Alex doesn't work at all. He's only 9, so he's still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! That makes sense - Alex is in primary school and not working.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

This means checking whether Alex is receiving benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name (not yours). 

For a 9-year-old, this would be very unusual. Can you confirm that Alex is not receiving any benefits in his own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just a kid!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that's what I expected! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This is another check that typically applies to older teenagers (16+), but I need to verify. Since Alex is only 9 and in primary school, I assume he's not in an apprenticeship. Can you confirm that?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's only 9 and still in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! That's clear. Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we're moving on to questions about **you** as the claimant.

**Residency question: Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of these apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** - this is the most common situation
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient financial resources
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do you normally live in the UK, or does one of these other situations apply to you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirement by normally living in the UK (in Manchester).

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can meet this requirement if **ANY** of these apply:

1. **You live with Alex** - this is the most common situation
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds Child Benefit

**Do you live with Alex, or do you contribute to his upkeep in one of these ways?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You live with Alex, so you meet the responsibility requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question: Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child. 

- If no one else is claiming, we can continue
- If someone else is claiming but you've agreed they'll transfer it to you, that's fine
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

**Is anyone else (such as Alex's other parent or a family member) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! No one else is claiming for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question: What is Alex's living situation?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply:

- Is Alex currently **in hospital**?
- Is Alex **in local authority care**?
- Is Alex **fostered**?
- Or is Alex living normally at home with you?

Based on what you've told me (Alex lives at home with you), I assume it's the normal living situation, but please confirm if any of these special circumstances apply.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, none of those apply - Alex just lives at home with me normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex lives at home with you normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question: Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the local council is not contributing to the costs of Alex's living expenses or care, correct?

Since Alex lives at home with you normally, I expect the answer is that the council is not paying for Alex's care. Can you confirm that?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me proceed to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for Alex. Based on our assessment:

✅ **Alex qualifies** - He's 9 years old (under 16), not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship

✅ **You meet the residency requirement** - You normally live in the UK (Manchester)

✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - He lives at home with you

✅ **No competing claims** - No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

✅ **No disqualifying circumstances** - Alex lives normally at home and the council isn't paying for his care

---

## 📝 Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

---

## 💰 Important Information:

- **Payments are made every 4 weeks** (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
- **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through your tax return. You can still claim it for National Insurance credit purposes even if you choose not to receive payments.

---

**For more information and to apply, visit:**
https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives at home with the claimant. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), has responsibility for Alex by living with them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 9-year-old child Alex. The child is under 16 and living at home normally, and the claimant is UK-resident and responsible for the child with no competing claims or disqualifying circumstances.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 9 years old (under 16), not in paid work, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and lives at home with the claimant. The claimant normally lives in the UK (Manchester), has responsibility for Alex by living with them, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex lives normally at home, and the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 9-year-old child Alex. The child is under 16 and living at home normally, and the claimant is UK-resident and responsible for the child with no competing claims or disqualifying circumstances.
